loop_cards.who_has: Who has the condition that stops a recursive subroutine?
Who has the error caused when the stopping condition is missing or never reached?
Who has the structure onto which too many frames are pushed?
Who has the region of visibility where a variable can be accessed?
Who has the variable visible only inside the subroutine that declares it?
Who has the variable visible throughout the whole program?
Who has a subroutine that performs a task and returns a single value?
Who has a subroutine that performs a task but returns no value?
Who has passing a copy of an argument so the original is unchanged?
Who has passing the memory address so the original can be changed?
Who has the OOP principle of bundling data and methods while keeping attributes private?
Who has the OOP principle of a subclass acquiring a superclass's attributes and methods?
Who has the OOP principle of the same method call behaving differently for different object types?
Who has building a solution step by step and returning to the last choice on a dead end?
Who has a rule of thumb giving a good-enough but not optimal answer quickly?
Who has a subroutine that calls itself, needing a base case and a recursive case?

callers.clue: Instructions executed one after another, in order.
Choosing between paths based on a condition (if / switch).
A loop repeating a fixed, known number of times (for...next).
A loop repeating until a condition changes (while / do...until).
A subroutine that calls itself, with a base case and a recursive case.
The condition that stops recursion, with no further recursive call.
The structure of frames holding parameters, locals and return addresses for active calls.
The error from too many pushed frames, e.g. a missing base case.
The region of a program where a variable can be accessed.
A variable accessible only within the subroutine that declares it.
A variable accessible throughout the whole program.
A subroutine that performs a task and returns a single value.
A subroutine that performs a task but returns no value.
Passing a copy of an argument, so the original is unaffected.
A template or blueprint defining the attributes and methods of its objects.
A specific instance of a class, created at run time.

quickfire.clue: A while loop tests its condition here, so the body may run zero times.
A do...until loop guarantees the body runs at least this many times.
Each recursive call pushes one of these onto the call stack.
The variable in a subroutine definition that receives an argument.
The actual value supplied at the point of the call.
A local of the same name does this to a global inside its subroutine.
OCR's keyword for the constructor method that runs on object creation.
A subclass replacing an inherited method with the same name and parameters.
Same method name but different parameter lists.
Searching large data sets for hidden patterns, e.g. basket analysis.
Splitting a process into stages so different items are processed at once.
Repeatedly splitting a problem into smaller instances, solving and combining.
